name: ai-attachment-card
title: AI Attachment Card
description: Reviewable attachment row showing type, name, size, upload and analysis status, a thumbnail slot, and remove or retry actions.

props.name: name
props.description: File name. Truncated in the row; the full name is on the native title.

props.name: size
props.description: Size in bytes. Formatted for display.

props.name: status
props.description: Lifecycle of the attachment. `ready` means available for a human to read.

props.name: thumbnail
props.description: Thumbnail slot — an , a page preview, or nothing.

props.name: note
props.description: Neutral processing facts only, e.g. "4 pages · text extracted". Never a finding.

props.name: error
props.description: What went wrong, shown in the error state.

props.name: actions
props.description: Extra caller-owned actions, e.g. "Open in viewer".

clinicalNotes: There is no findings slot on this card, and that absence is the design. A wound photo or an ECG strip is exactly the kind of attachment a product is tempted to annotate with a one-line verdict — "looks infected", "possible ST elevation" — and a verdict rendered as a caption on a thumbnail arrives with no evidence, no limitations, and no review action attached to it. Anything a model concluded from an attachment belongs in a sourced answer a clinician can interrogate; `note` is for neutral processing facts ("4 pages · text extracted", "read for text and metadata") and nothing else. The status enum keeps the two questions a reviewer actually asks separate — did the file arrive, and has anything read it yet — because "uploaded" and "understood" are not the same state, and an attachment that failed silently is an attachment the clinician believes the assistant saw. Removal is always available: a misfiled document must be retractable before it becomes part of the record.
notes: Pairs with ai-attachment-picker: the picker hands back File[], the caller renders one card per file and owns the upload/analysis state machine. Bytes are formatted locally (B/KB/MB/GB).
